title: Famous People Who Made a Difference
subject: history

content.text:
Throughout history, some people have done brave and amazing things that changed the world for the better!

🦅 George Washington (1732–1799)
George Washington was the first President of the United States. He helped lead American colonists to freedom during the Revolutionary War. He believed everyone deserved a fair government.

✊ Harriet Tubman (1822–1913)
Harriet Tubman was incredibly brave. She escaped from slavery and then risked her life to help hundreds of other enslaved people escape to freedom through a secret network called the Underground Railroad.

💡 Abraham Lincoln (1809–1865)
Abraham Lincoln was the 16th President. During a very difficult time called the Civil War, he worked to end slavery in America and keep the country united.

🌟 Rosa Parks (1913–2005)
Rosa Parks stood up for fairness by refusing to give up her seat on a bus in 1955. Her courage helped start the Civil Rights Movement, which fought for equal rights for all people.

🔬 Marie Curie (1867–1934)
Marie Curie was a brilliant scientist who discovered two new elements and won two Nobel Prizes. She showed that women could be great scientists.

These people were brave, fair, and determined. Their choices made life better for many others. We can honor them by being brave and fair ourselves!

diagrams.alt: Illustrated portraits of George Washington, Harriet Tubman, Rosa Parks, and Abraham Lincoln

readingPassage.title: Rosa Parks: One Brave Seat
readingPassage.text:
It was December 1, 1955, in Montgomery, Alabama. A woman named Rosa Parks sat down on a city bus after a long day of work.

In those days, there were unfair rules. Black passengers had to sit in the back of the bus. If the bus got full, they had to give up their seats to white passengers.

When the driver told Rosa to give up her seat, she said no.

Rosa was arrested and taken to jail. But instead of making people afraid, her bravery inspired them. A young minister named Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. helped organize a boycott. For 381 days, Black citizens in Montgomery refused to ride the buses. They walked, carpooled, and took taxis instead.

The boycott worked. The Supreme Court ruled that the unfair bus rules were against the law.

Rosa Parks once said, "I would like to be remembered as a person who wanted to be free and wanted others to also be free."

Rosa's one brave moment changed history. She showed that one person can make a big difference — even by simply refusing to give up a seat.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What happened when Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat?

options.text: She was arrested and taken to jail
The driver gave her a medal

comprehensionQuestions.hint: Read the paragraph right after Rosa says no — what happened next?

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the Montgomery Bus Boycott?

options.text: Black citizens refusing to ride buses for 381 days

comprehensionQuestions.hint: Find the paragraph that describes what happened after Rosa was arrested.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did Rosa Parks want to be remembered for?

options.text: Wanting to be free and wanting others to be free

comprehensionQuestions.hint: Look for the quote from Rosa Parks in the passage.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What lesson does the story of Rosa Parks teach us?

options.text: One person's bravery can make a big difference

comprehensionQuestions.hint: Read the very last paragraph of the passage.

questions.prompt: Who was the first President of the United States?

options.text: A secret network that helped enslaved people escape to freedom

questions.prompt: What was Abraham Lincoln's important achievement?

options.text: He worked to end slavery and keep the country united

questions.prompt: What is one way we can honor famous people from history?

options.text: By being brave and fair ourselves
